title: ATM Fraud Pattern Detection
app: ATM switch logs, core banking TA, ISO 8583 parsers
spl:
index=payments sourcetype IN ("atm:transaction","iso8583:auth") txn_type="withdrawal"
| eval hour=strftime(_time,"%H")
| stats count, dc(atm_id) as atms, values(card_hash) as cards by src_merchant_id, hour
| where count>20 AND atms>5
| sort -count
description: Correlates card-present withdrawals with geographic velocity and device tamper signals to catch skimming and cash-out campaigns before losses spread.
value: Correlates card-present withdrawals with geographic velocity and device tamper signals to catch skimming and cash-out campaigns before losses spread.
implementation: Ingest ATM host and authorization logs with terminal ID, amount, response code, and geo. Baseline per-terminal throughput; alert on velocity across many ATMs or impossible travel vs. last chip read.
visualization: Table (suspicious terminals), Map (withdrawal clusters), Line chart (ATM fraud attempts over time).
knownFalsePositives: Spikes during high-volume retail or holiday periods, legitimate batch chargebacks, bank test transactions, or ATM fleet maintenance and cash loading.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: ATM switch logs, core banking TA, ISO 8583 parsers.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="atm:transaction"`, `sourcetype="iso8583:auth"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest ATM host and authorization logs with terminal ID, amount, response code, and geo. Baseline per-terminal throughput; alert on velocity across many ATMs or impossible travel vs. last chip read.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=payments sourcetype IN ("atm:transaction","iso8583:auth") txn_type="withdrawal"
| eval hour=strftime(_time,"%H")
| stats count, dc(atm_id) as atms, values(card_hash) as cards by src_merchant_id, hour
| where count>20 AND atms>5
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**ATM Fraud Pattern Detection** — Correlates card-present withdrawals with geographic velocity and device tamper signals to catch skimming and cash-out campaigns before losses spread.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="atm:transaction"`, `sourcetype="iso8583:auth"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): ATM switch logs, core banking TA, ISO 8583 parsers. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: payments.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=payments. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hour** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src_merchant_id, hour** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count>20 AND atms>5` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (suspicious terminals), Map (withdrawal clusters), Line chart (ATM fraud attempts over time).
grandmaExplanation: In Financial Services, we pay close attention to "ATM Fraud Pattern Detection" in our day-to-day work so that fraud, waste, and compliance issues do not slip by quietly. We help teams focus on the few situations that need a person, not every routine blip in the data.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable for the primary search: it relies on application- and industry-specific log formats, not a normalized CIM data model. Optional mapping to CIM, if you adopt it, is a separate follow-on project.
